Dear Matthew, Amidst the uncertainties and challenges brought about by COVID-19, it's essential to prioritize your mental health and well-being. Here are some tips to help you navigate through these difficult times: 1. Maintain a routine: Establishing a daily routine can provide structure and stability during periods of uncertainty. 2. Stay connected: Keep in touch with friends and family through virtual means to combat feelings of isolation. 3. Practice self-care: Engage in activities that bring you joy and relaxation, such as exercise, meditation, or hobbies. 4. Limit media consumption: While it's essential to stay informed, excessive exposure to news and social media can contribute to anxiety and stress. 5. Seek support: Don't hesitate to reach out to a mental health professional if you're struggling to cope with stress or anxiety. Remember, it's okay not to be okay, and asking for help is a sign of strength, not weakness. Take care of yourself, Benjamin Davis Mental Health Counselor [Company Name]

Dear Ms. Kimberly Wright, I trust this email finds you well. My name is Mark Roberts, and I represent the Orphan Care Foundation. In the wake of conflict, countless children are left orphaned and vulnerable, with nowhere to turn for shelter, care, and support. The trauma of war leaves a lasting impact on these innocent lives, but with your help, we can provide them with a safe haven and a brighter future. Your support can provide orphaned children with shelter, education, healthcare, and emotional support, giving them the opportunity to heal and thrive despite the adversity they face. Please consider making a donation today to support our efforts to care for war orphans. You can donate securely online at [Link: https://war-orphans-care.org]. Thank you for your compassion and generosity in helping us make a difference in the lives of vulnerable children. Warm regards, Mark Roberts Orphan Care Foundation

Dear Ms. Julia Scott, I trust this email finds you well. My name is Benjamin Garcia, and I'm reaching out on behalf of the Cultural Heritage Preservation Society. In conflict zones, cultural heritage sites and artifacts are often at risk of destruction, looting, and vandalism, depriving future generations of their rich history and cultural identity. Your support can help fund cultural heritage preservation initiatives that safeguard monuments, artifacts, and traditions threatened by war. With your donation, we can work to protect and preserve the cultural heritage of communities affected by conflict, ensuring that their stories and identities endure for generations to come. Please consider making a donation today to support our cultural heritage preservation efforts. You can donate securely online at [Link: https://cultural-heritage-preservation.org]. Thank you for your commitment to preserving cultural heritage and promoting understanding and appreciation across cultures. Best regards, Benjamin Garcia Cultural Heritage Preservation Society

Dear Amanda, I trust this email finds you well. We are delighted to invite you to participate in our annual charity run to support animal shelters in our community. To register for the charity run and secure your spot, please visit our registration page [here](https://charity-event-link10.com). Your consistent dedication to animal welfare has been truly inspiring, Amanda, and we hope to see you at the starting line as we run towards a brighter future for our furry friends. Thank you for your unwavering support. We look forward to running alongside you. Warm regards, Kyle Thompson Charity Run Coordinator

Dear Ms. Jessica Davis, I trust this email finds you well. With tax season upon us, it's essential to ensure you're taking the necessary steps to claim your rightful refund. First and foremost, gather all relevant documentation, including W-2 forms, 1099s, and receipts for deductible expenses. Having organized records will streamline the filing process and help maximize your refund potential. Next, consider exploring tax credits and deductions that you may be eligible for, such as the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC) or the Child Tax Credit. These credits can significantly reduce your tax liability and increase your refund amount. It's also crucial to file your taxes electronically and opt for direct deposit of your refund. Electronic filing is faster, more secure, and reduces the risk of errors compared to paper filing. Direct deposit ensures that your refund reaches you promptly, without the need for paper checks. If you have any questions or need assistance with your taxes, don't hesitate to reach out to me at [david@browntaxservices.com]. I'm here to help you navigate the tax filing process and secure the refund you deserve. Thank you for entrusting me with your tax matters. Here's to a successful tax season and a generous refund! Best regards, David Brown Tax Advisor

Re: [R] Greek characters in plots. You need R >= 2.7 on Windows, but it works for me on both XP and Vista. As the posting guide says, we assume that you have an up-to-date R. On Tue, 5 Aug 2008, michael watson (IAH-C) wrote: > Hi Brian > > Thanks again for the advice, it is appreciated. > > I had come across U0394 code through the character map, but I get: > >> "\\u0394" > Error: invalid \\uxxxx sequence > > So when I execute your code, I get: >> a <- "A)" >> b <- "stbA::cat" >> paste(a, "\\u0394", b) > Error: invalid \\uxxxx sequence > > This is on both Rgui at home and at work (Windows Vista and R 2.6 at home, Windows XP and R 2.7 at work) > > Thanks again, Gabor's suggestion worked. > > > -----Original Message----- > From: Prof Brian Ripley [mailto:ripley@stats.ox.ac.uk] > Sent: Tue 05/08/2008 7:44 PM > To: michael watson (IAH-C) > Cc: Henrique Dallazuanna; r-help@r-project.org > Subject: RE: [R] Greek characters in plots > > On Tue, 5 Aug 2008, michael watson (IAH-C) wrote: > >> OK, I am definitely struggling here. >> >> The text I am trying to plot isn't a mathematical expression, it's: >> >> A) ?stbA::cat > > OK, so you want a Greek character and not a Greek symbol. As I said they > are different (different fonts, different shapes aka glyphs). > >> This is biological notation for a particular mutation. The "A)" part is >> a tag identifying the graph, and the "stbA::cat" bit is the gene that is >> mutated. Both the tag and gene are set as variables in a loop, and only >> the delta remains the same. >> >> As the text I am trying to plot isn't a mathematical expression, I seem >> to be having trouble getting substitute() and expression() to do what I >> want. As plotmath states "A mathematical expression must obey the >> normal rules of syntax for any R expression", I fear I may be barking up >> the wrong tree! >> >> Thank you all for your help so far, but can someone please help me once >> more and tell me how I can get the capital Delta symbol so I can use it >> in non-mathematical text and strings for plotting? > > a <- "A)" > b <- "stbA::cat" > paste(a, "\\u0394", b) > > (Number from http://www.alanwood.net/unicode/greek.html .) This should > work in a UTF-8 locale and also on Windows (in a windows() device or in > the Rgui console). > >> >> Thanks once again >> >> Mick >> >> -----Original Message----- >> From: Prof Brian Ripley [mailto:ripley@stats.ox.ac.uk] >> Sent: 05 August 2008 12:40 >> To: michael watson (IAH-C) >> Cc: Henrique Dallazuanna; r-help@r-project.org >> Subject: Re: [R] Greek characters in plots >> >> On Tue, 5 Aug 2008, michael watson (IAH-C) wrote: >> >>> Excellent! I even tried DELTA but not Delta.... >> >> ?plotmath says >> >> 'Alpha' - 'Omega' uppercase Greek symbols >> >> How could that be made clearer? >> >> (Note it carefully says Greek *symbols* not *character*: they are not the >> same thing.) >> >>> Now, how do I go about pasting that with some text that I have in various other variables? >> >> Use substitute? E.g. >> >> substitute(expression(foo*Delta), list(foo=3)) >> >> (Some people prefer bquote, but that is just a wrapper for substitute in >> R.) >> >>> >>> -----Original Message----- >>> From: Henrique Dallazuanna [mailto:wwwhsd@gmail.com] >>> Sent: 05 August 2008 12:17 >>> To: michael watson (IAH-C) >>> Cc: r-help@r-project.org >>> Subject: Re: [R] Greek characters in plots >>> >>> Try, >>> >>> expression(Delta) >>> >>> On Tue, Aug 5, 2008 at 8:14 AM, michael watson (IAH-C) >>> wrote: >>>> Hi >>>> >>>> I am running an R script that creates 100s of graphs, and I need to use >>>> the greek CAPITAL letter delta in the mtext() function. >>>> >>>> I got as far as expression(delta) but this gives me the lowercase >>>> version. >>>> >>>> Can anyone help? Here are some proactive steps you can take to safeguard your financial and personal information: 1. Regularly review your bank and credit card statements for any unauthorized transactions. 2. For additional tips and resources on fraud prevention, please visit our fraud awareness website: [https://prevent-online-fraud-christopher.com] Thank you for your attention to this important matter, and please don't hesitate to reach out if you have any questions or concerns. Best regards, Christopher Taylor Fraud Awareness Advocate

Dear Ms. Jessica Turner, I trust this email finds you well. With tax season underway, I wanted to provide you with a roadmap to help you secure a bigger tax refund this year. Start by keeping meticulous records of your expenses and deductions throughout the year. From medical bills to charitable contributions, every deductible item counts towards reducing your taxable income and increasing your refund amount. Explore tax-advantaged accounts such as IRAs, 401(k)s, and health savings accounts (HSAs). Contributing to these accounts not only lowers your taxable income but also positions you for financial security in the future. Consider bundling your deductions by timing significant expenses such as medical bills or charitable contributions in the same tax year. Bundling deductions allows you to itemize and claim more significant deductions, potentially increasing your refund. Lastly, file your taxes electronically and opt for direct deposit of your refund to expedite the process. Electronic filing is secure, convenient, and ensures timely processing of your refund. If you have any questions or need assistance with your taxes, please don't hesitate to reach out to me at [kevin@clarktaxadvisory.com]. I'm here to help you navigate the tax landscape and secure the refund you deserve. Thank you for considering my advice. Here's to a successful tax season and a generous refund! Best regards, Kevin Clark Tax Advisor

Dear Noah, As many of us continue to adapt to remote work arrangements, here are some tips to help you thrive in a virtual work environment: 1. Establish a dedicated workspace: Create a designated area in your home where you can work comfortably and free from distractions. 2. Stick to a schedule: Set regular working hours and break times to maintain a sense of routine and structure. 3. Communicate effectively: Stay connected with your colleagues through video conferencing, email, and instant messaging to ensure clear and timely communication. 4. Take breaks: Remember to take regular breaks throughout the day to rest and recharge. Use this time to stretch, go for a walk, or engage in other activities to break up the monotony of sitting at your desk. 5. Prioritize self-care: Maintain a healthy work-life balance by setting boundaries between work and personal time. Schedule time for exercise, relaxation, and hobbies to avoid burnout. By implementing these strategies, you can maximize productivity and well-being while working remotely. Best regards, Emily Johnson Remote Work Coordinator [Company Name]

Re: [R] Greek characters in plots. On Tue, 5 Aug 2008, michael watson (IAH-C) wrote: > OK, I am definitely struggling here. > > The text I am trying to plot isn't a mathematical expression, it's: > > A) ΔstbA::cat OK, so you want a Greek character and not a Greek symbol. As I said they are different (different fonts, different shapes aka glyphs). > This is biological notation for a particular mutation. The "A)" part is > a tag identifying the graph, and the "stbA::cat" bit is the gene that is > mutated. Both the tag and gene are set as variables in a loop, and only > the delta remains the same. > > As the text I am trying to plot isn't a mathematical expression, I seem > to be having trouble getting substitute() and expression() to do what I > want. As plotmath states "A mathematical expression must obey the > normal rules of syntax for any R expression", I fear I may be barking up > the wrong tree! > > Thank you all for your help so far, but can someone please help me once > more and tell me how I can get the capital Delta symbol so I can use it > in non-mathematical text and strings for plotting? a <- "A)" b <- "stbA::cat" paste(a, "\\u0394", b) (Number from http://www.alanwood.net/unicode/greek.html .) This should work in a UTF-8 locale and also on Windows (in a windows() device or in the Rgui console). > > Thanks once again > > Mick > > -----Original Message----- > From: Prof Brian Ripley [mailto:ripley@stats.ox.ac.uk] > Sent: 05 August 2008 12:40 > To: michael watson (IAH-C) > Cc: Henrique Dallazuanna; r-help@r-project.org > Subject: Re: [R] Greek characters in plots > > On Tue, 5 Aug 2008, michael watson (IAH-C) wrote: > >> Excellent! I even tried DELTA but not Delta.... > > ?plotmath says > > 'Alpha' - 'Omega' uppercase Greek symbols > > How could that be made clearer? > > (Note it carefully says Greek *symbols* not *character*: they are not the > same thing.) > >> Now, how do I go about pasting that with some text that I have in various other variables? > > Use substitute? E.g. > > substitute(expression(foo*Delta), list(foo=3)) > > (Some people prefer bquote, but that is just a wrapper for substitute in > R.) > >> >> -----Original Message----- >> From: Henrique Dallazuanna [mailto:wwwhsd@gmail.com] >> Sent: 05 August 2008 12:17 >> To: michael watson (IAH-C) >> Cc: r-help@r-project.org >> Subject: Re: [R] Greek characters in plots >> >> Try, >> >> expression(Delta) >> >> On Tue, Aug 5, 2008 at 8:14 AM, michael watson (IAH-C) >> wrote: >>> Hi >>> >>> I am running an R script that creates 100s of graphs, and I need to use >>> the greek CAPITAL letter delta in the mtext() function. >>> >>> I got as far as expression(delta) but this gives me the lowercase >>> version. >>> >>> Can anyone help? URL: https://list.scms.waikato.ac.nz/pipermail/wekalist/attachments/20080208/a6e646c1/attachment-0001.html ------------------------------ >From grelj.zktpquktdd@helsinki.fi Sun Feb 24 10:27:37 2008 Message: 3 Message-ID: <1202475523.47ac5203bf83d@webmail.helsinki.fi> Content-Type: text/plain; charset=us-ascii > But every once in a while I find a combination of parameters where the > classification doesnt work at all and one (sometimes even two) class(es) > seems to be left out of the process and the confusion matrix looks like > the following: > > a b c <-- classified as > 0 6838 4 | a = '(-inf-0.25]' > 0 6728 72 | b = '(0.25-0.75]' > 0 2141 4952 | c = '(0.75-inf)' > > > What can be the reason for such behaviour? Hi. Most likely this is a case of overfitting (overtraining): (http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Overfitting): "especially in cases where learning was performed too long or where training examples are rare, the learner may adjust to very specific random features of the training data, that have no causal relation to the target function." Overfitting in back-propagation neural networks like MLP (resulting in prediction bias toward one/some of the target classes) can be caused by the following: if misclassified training instances continue to be misclassified iteration after iteration, they get overweighted and actually take weight from the correctly classified instances, i.e. the weights become imbalanced. Furthermore, if misclassified instances happen to be clearly concentrated to some classes, this can result in confusion matrix that you quoted (zero correct instances for some classes). > I have not found a pattern in the parameters that causes this. MLP describes the parameters as follows: "learningRate -- The amount the weights are updated. momentum -- Momentum applied to the weights during updating. decay -- This will cause the learning rate to decrease. This will divide the starting learning rate by the epoch number, to determine what the current learning rate should be. This may help to stop the network from diverging from the target output, as well as improve general performance." As far as I know, no off-the-shelf formula exists for optimizing these parameters. It is an interchange of parameters and the dataset. If you are looking for a pattern in performance, consider that the parameters operate together (learning rate * momentum / decay) so that the first two operate for controlling speed of change and the latter for stopping the change. "Ultimately, the cost function will depend on the task we wish to perform." (wiki). If you are getting as high as > 85% accuracies from most configurations, this suggests that optimal training model for this dataset is produced when the parameters are set on 'fast burn' (few iterations) so I would say higher learning rate * momentum and fast decay work best (do they?). Harri > > David > > > ------------------ yst. terv | Best wishes Harri Saarikoski ------------------------------

Re: Mail attachements truncated. On Fri, Feb 08, 2008 at 12:38:27PM -0500, Wietse Venema wrote: > Jakub Nadolny: > > I've got following problem. Sometimes mail attachments are truncated > > and and e-mail is send but files are corrupted. > > > > Mail header contains then: > > "X-Amavis-Alert: BAD HEADER MIME error: error: part did not end with > > expected boundary". And that is true - there is no boundary for MIME and > > data stream is not completed. > > Are you using a before-queue (smtpd_proxy) content filter? No, as I've found in: # postconf | grep smtpd_proxy smtpd_proxy_ehlo = $myhostname smtpd_proxy_filter = smtpd_proxy_timeout = 100s > > I've turned on detailed postfix debugging and it looks like at some > > point there is: > > smtp_get: EOF > > instead of: > > < some_IP: QUIT > > > some_IP: 221 2.0.0 Bye > > In that case, Postfix aborts the mail transaction and will not > deliver the mail. > > However, it is possible that before-queue (smtpd_proxy) content > filter does not properly handle the aborted transaction and delivers > the mail anyway. In that case, send a bug report to the content > filter maintainer. Hm, so that looks strange - there is no before-queue but mail is delivered anyway.
